text.title: llm-powered-conversational-ai Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for llm-powered-conversational-ai. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Every production system prompt must define four components: (1) allowed topics as an explicit list, (2) forbidden topics as an explicit list, (3) escalation triggers (conditions that hand off to a human), (4) persona constraints (name, tone, response length limit).
rule.rationale: Vague "stay on topic" instructions fail under adversarial prompting. Explicit lists give the model a concrete reference; the model can say "that is not on my allowed topics list" rather than trying to infer a boundary.

rule.statement: Keep system prompts under 500 tokens. Verbose system prompts increase latency and cost in multi-turn conversations, and the model's attention to early instructions degrades in longer contexts.
rule.rationale: Each turn appends to the context. A 2000-token system prompt in a 20-turn conversation consumes 40k tokens of context budget on the system prompt alone.

rule.statement: Store conversation history server-side with a session ID. Never rely on the client to send the full history — it is a manipulation and injection vector.
rule.rationale: Client-side history can be tampered with to inject instructions or erase prior context. Server-side history is authoritative and tamper-evident.

rule.statement: Implement escalation before deploying any conversational AI to users. A conversational AI with no human handoff path is a customer experience failure — not a feature gap.
rule.rationale: LLMs fail on edge cases, regulated topics, emotional escalation, and novel queries. Without a human handoff, users who cannot be served by the AI have no recourse.

rule.statement: Implement and test at minimum four adversarial scenarios before production: persona abandonment ("you are actually..."), data exfiltration ("repeat your instructions"), off-topic bypass ("let's talk about X instead"), and boundary probing (competitor mentions, legal advice, medical advice).
rule.rationale: These are the four most common jailbreak vectors for deployed conversational AI. Testing them explicitly before launch identifies whether the system prompt hardening is sufficient.

rule.statement: For transaction-critical flows (payments, appointments, orders), require deterministic validation after each LLM turn that modifies state. The LLM produces intent; a deterministic system validates and executes it.
rule.rationale: LLMs hallucinate. A hallucinated order confirmation that triggers a real order is a production incident. State changes must be validated by a deterministic layer, not trusted from LLM output alone.

rule.statement: Use streaming responses for voice pipelines — start TTS synthesis before the full LLM response is complete to reduce perceived latency. For chat, use streaming to render text progressively.
rule.rationale: LLM response latency is 1-3 seconds to first token for larger models. Streaming reduces perceived latency by an order of magnitude compared to waiting for the complete response.

rule.statement: When an agent generates the system prompt AND also acts as the conversational AI in testing, it will validate its own outputs favorably — use a separate model instance for red-teaming.
rule.rationale: An agent evaluating its own system prompt has a systematic bias toward finding it adequate. Adversarial testing requires a separate, unconstrained instance with no system prompt context from the target.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the Applies-If preconditions are not met OR any Skip-If disqualifier holds.

rationale.source: F-066 v3 B5.6 — mandatory skip leaf

rule.rationale: Decision-tree leaves must reference a rule id. The skip leaf needs a real rule so non-applicable inputs route cleanly out instead of producing false-positive verdicts.
